questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content:
x ← 5
y ← 3
DISPLAY(x + y)

questions.question: What is displayed when the code segment above is executed?

choices.explanation: x is assigned 5 and y is assigned 3 — DISPLAY(x + y) adds them and displays 8.
53 would result from string concatenation — the + operator with numbers performs addition, giving 8.
2 would result from subtraction (5 - 3) — the code uses addition (+), not subtraction.

choices.text: x + y
choices.explanation: DISPLAY evaluates the expression and shows its value — it does not display the literal text 'x + y'.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content:
x ← 10
x ← x + 5
DISPLAY(x)

questions.question: What is displayed when the code segment is executed?

choices.explanation: 10 is the initial value of x — the second assignment updates x to x + 5 = 15.
x starts at 10; x ← x + 5 replaces x with 10+5=15; DISPLAY(x) shows 15.
5 is the value added to x, not the final value — after x ← x + 5, x becomes 15.
DISPLAY evaluates the expression — it displays the numeric value 15, not the text 'x + 5'.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the purpose of the assignment operator (←) in College Board pseudocode?

choices.text: It tests whether two values are equal
choices.explanation: Testing equality uses = in conditions — the ← operator stores a value into a variable.

choices.text: It displays a value to the output
choices.explanation: DISPLAY() outputs values — ← stores a value into a variable.

choices.text: It stores the value of the expression on the right into the variable on the left
choices.explanation: a ← expression evaluates the right-hand expression and stores the result in the variable a.

choices.text: It adds two numbers together
choices.explanation: Addition uses + — ← is the assignment operator that stores a value into a variable.

stimulus.content:
a ← 4
b ← 7
a ← b
DISPLAY(a)

choices.explanation: 4 was a's original value — after a ← b, a now holds b's value of 7.

choices.text: ab
choices.explanation: DISPLAY outputs a numeric value, not the letter concatenation 'ab'.
11 would result from a + b — but a ← b replaces a with b's value, it does not add them.
a ← b copies b's value (7) into a — DISPLAY(a) shows 7.

choices.text: yes
choices.explanation: 5 > 3 is TRUE, so the IF block executes and displays 'yes'.

choices.text: no
choices.explanation: 'no' is in the ELSE block — since 5 > 3 is TRUE, the ELSE block is skipped.

choices.text: yes no
choices.explanation: Only one branch executes in an IF/ELSE — since the condition is TRUE, only 'yes' is displayed.

choices.text: Nothing is displayed
choices.explanation: The condition 5 > 3 is TRUE, so the IF block executes and 'yes' is displayed.
0 is the initial value of count — the REPEAT loop runs 4 times, incrementing count each time to 4.
count starts at 0 and is incremented by 1 four times: 0→1→2→3→4. DISPLAY(count) shows 4.
1 would be the value after one iteration — the loop runs 4 times, so count reaches 4.
5 would require 5 iterations — the loop runs exactly 4 TIMES, resulting in count = 4.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: In College Board pseudocode, list indices start at which value?

choices.explanation: 0-based indexing is common in many programming languages (like Python and Java), but College Board pseudocode lists are 1-indexed.
-1 is not a valid starting index in College Board pseudocode.
College Board pseudocode uses 1-based indexing — the first element is at index 1, not 0.

choices.text: The index depends on the list size
choices.explanation: The starting index is always 1 in College Board pseudocode — it does not depend on list size.

stimulus.content:
myList ← [10, 20, 30, 40, 50]
DISPLAY(myList[3])

choices.explanation: 20 is at index 2 — in 1-indexed CB pseudocode, myList[3] is the third element, which is 30.
10 is at index 1 — myList[3] in 1-indexed CB pseudocode is 30.
40 is at index 4 — myList[3] in 1-indexed pseudocode is 30, not 40.
College Board lists are 1-indexed: myList[1]=10, myList[2]=20, myList[3]=30. DISPLAY(myList[3]) shows 30.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What does LENGTH(myList) return if myList contains [5, 10, 15, 20]?

choices.explanation: LENGTH returns the number of elements in the list — myList has 4 elements (5, 10, 15, 20), so LENGTH(myList) = 4.
5 is the first element's value, not the list length — LENGTH counts elements, not their values.
20 is the last element's value — LENGTH counts the number of items in the list, which is 4.
1 is not the length — LENGTH([5, 10, 15, 20]) returns 4 because there are 4 elements.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes a Boolean expression?

choices.text: An expression that always results in a whole number
choices.explanation: Whole number results are numeric expressions — Boolean expressions evaluate to either TRUE or FALSE.

choices.text: An expression that evaluates to either TRUE or FALSE
choices.explanation: Boolean expressions use comparison and logical operators — they always produce one of exactly two values: TRUE or FALSE.

choices.text: An expression that contains only numbers
choices.explanation: Numeric expressions contain only numbers — Boolean expressions use comparisons (>, <, =) or logic (AND, OR, NOT) to produce TRUE/FALSE.

choices.text: An expression that displays output to the user
choices.explanation: DISPLAY() produces output — a Boolean expression is a comparison or logical operation that evaluates to TRUE or FALSE.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What is displayed when the code segment is executed?

choices.text: in range out of range
choices.explanation: IF/ELSE executes only one branch — only 'in range' is displayed because the condition is true.

choices.text: out of range
choices.explanation: 'out of range' only displays when both conditions are not both true — since 8>5 AND 8<10 are both true, 'in range' displays.

choices.text: in range
choices.explanation: x=8: (8>5) is TRUE AND (8<10) is TRUE. TRUE AND TRUE = TRUE, so 'in range' is displayed.

choices.text: Nothing is displayed
choices.explanation: The condition (8>5 AND 8<10) is TRUE — the IF block executes and 'in range' is displayed.
5 is the last element in the list — the loop sums all elements: 1+2+3+4+5=15.
10 is the sum of 1+2+3+4 — the loop includes 5 as well, giving total = 15.
1 is the first element — after iterating through all elements, total equals the sum 15.
FOR EACH iterates through all elements: total = 0+1+2+3+4+5 = 15. DISPLAY(total) shows 15.
Tracing: x=10 (10>0, loop)→7 (7>0, loop)→4 (4>0, loop)→1 (1>0, loop)→-2 (-2≤0, stop). DISPLAY(-2).
x never reaches exactly 0 — starting at 10 and decrementing by 3: 10, 7, 4, 1, -2. The loop stops when x ≤ 0, giving -2.
Tracing: x=10→7→4→1→-2. The loop continues as long as x > 0. After x becomes 1, the condition x≤0 is still FALSE, so another iteration runs: x becomes 1-3=-2. Display is -2.
-3 is not the sequence value — subtracting 3 from 1 gives -2, not -3.
7 is the input argument — the procedure multiplies it by 2 and returns 14.
double(7) calls the procedure with n=7; RETURN(7*2) returns 14; DISPLAY(14) shows 14.
2 is just the multiplier — the procedure returns n * 2 = 7 * 2 = 14.
DISPLAY evaluates the result of calling double(7) — it displays the numeric value 14, not the expression text.

stimulus.content:
myList ← [3, 6, 9]
APPEND(myList, 12)
DISPLAY(LENGTH(myList))

choices.explanation: 3 was the original list length — APPEND adds a new element, making the length 4.
12 is the value appended to the list — LENGTH returns the count of elements, which is 4.
myList starts with 3 elements. APPEND(myList, 12) adds 12, making it [3,6,9,12]. LENGTH is now 4.
5 would require adding two elements — only one APPEND call is made, so the length is 4.

stimulus.content:
n ← 17
DISPLAY(n MOD 5)

choices.explanation: 17 / 5 = 3 remainder 2 — the quotient is 3 but the MOD returns the remainder, which is 2.
0 would mean 17 is divisible by 5 — 17/5 has a remainder of 2, not 0.
5 is the divisor — MOD returns the remainder (2), not the divisor.
17 MOD 5 = remainder when 17 is divided by 5. 5×3=15, 17-15=2. The result is 2.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following best describes procedural abstraction?

choices.text: Using a named procedure to hide implementation details so the programmer only needs to know what it does, not how
choices.explanation: Procedural abstraction lets programmers call a procedure by name without knowing its internal code — they only need to know the procedure's name, parameters, and what it returns.

choices.text: Storing a list of values under a single variable name
choices.explanation: Storing multiple values in a list is data abstraction — procedural abstraction involves naming a reusable block of code.

choices.text: Translating a program from pseudocode to a real programming language
choices.explanation: Translating languages is compilation or interpretation — procedural abstraction is about encapsulating functionality behind a named procedure.

choices.text: Displaying the output of a calculation to the user
choices.explanation: Displaying output uses DISPLAY() — procedural abstraction is a design concept for managing complexity.
2 is the initial value of min — the loop finds smaller values. 1 < 2, so min is updated to 1.
The algorithm starts with min=2, then finds 1 < 2, updates min to 1. No other value is smaller than 1. DISPLAY(1).
8 is the largest value — the algorithm finds the minimum, so 1 is the correct answer.
5 is the maximum value in the list — the algorithm tracks the minimum, not the maximum.
9 would stop the loop only if n needed to equal exactly 10 — after n=9, the condition 9≥10 is FALSE, so another iteration runs: n becomes 12.
n never equals 10 in this sequence — starting at 0 and adding 3: 0, 3, 6, 9, 12. The loop stops when n≥10, giving 12.
n=0(loop)→3(loop)→6(loop)→9(loop)→12. After 12: 12≥10 is TRUE, loop stops. DISPLAY(12).
3 is after just one iteration — the loop continues until n≥10, reaching 12.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: A programmer wants to use binary search on a list of names to find 'Smith'. What must be true about the list first?

choices.text: The list must contain exactly 100 elements
choices.explanation: Binary search works on lists of any size — the requirement is that the list is sorted, not that it has a specific length.

choices.text: The list must have no duplicate values
choices.explanation: Binary search can work with duplicate values — the critical requirement is that the list is sorted.

choices.text: The list must contain only numbers
choices.explanation: Binary search works on any comparable data type, including strings — sorting can apply to names alphabetically.

choices.text: The list must be sorted in alphabetical order
choices.explanation: Binary search requires a sorted list — it works by comparing the target to the middle element and eliminating half the remaining options each step.

choices.text: false
choices.explanation: 9 is odd: 9 MOD 2 = 1, which is not 0, so the ELSE branch returns false. DISPLAY(false).

choices.text: true
choices.explanation: 9 MOD 2 = 1 (not 0), so the condition is FALSE — RETURN(false) executes.
9 is the argument — the procedure returns a Boolean (true/false), not the number itself.
1 is the value of 9 MOD 2, but it is used as a condition — the procedure returns a Boolean, not the MOD value.

stimulus.content:
myList ← [10, 20, 30, 40]
REMOVE(myList, 2)
DISPLAY(myList[2])

choices.explanation: 20 was at index 2 — REMOVE(myList, 2) removes it. After removal, what was at index 3 (30) shifts to index 2.
REMOVE(myList, 2) removes the element at index 2 (which is 20). The list becomes [10, 30, 40]. myList[2] is now 30.
40 was at index 4 and is now at index 3 after the remove — myList[2] is 30.
10 is at index 1 and is not affected by REMOVE(myList, 2) — the question asks for myList[2], which is now 30.

choices.text: Always the value 3 (the midpoint)
choices.explanation: RANDOM is not deterministic — it returns a randomly chosen value each time it is called.

choices.text: A random decimal number between 1 and 6
choices.explanation: RANDOM(a, b) in College Board pseudocode returns an integer — not a decimal.

choices.text: A random integer between 1 and 6, inclusive
choices.explanation: RANDOM(a, b) returns a random integer between a and b inclusive — RANDOM(1, 6) could return 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, or 6.

choices.text: Either 1 or 6, with no values in between
choices.explanation: RANDOM(1, 6) can return any integer from 1 through 6, not just the endpoints.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What is displayed when the code is executed with score = 85?

choices.text: A
choices.explanation: 85 < 90, so the first IF condition is FALSE — the program moves to the ELSE block.

choices.text: A B C
choices.explanation: Only one branch executes in nested IF/ELSE — since 85 >= 80 is true, only 'B' displays.

choices.text: C
choices.explanation: C displays only if score < 80 — since 85 >= 80 is TRUE, 'B' displays.

choices.text: B
choices.explanation: 85 < 90 (skip A), then 85 >= 80 is TRUE — the nested IF executes and displays 'B'.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What is always displayed, regardless of the random values?

choices.explanation: The loop runs exactly 5 times, appending one element each time — regardless of random values, LENGTH(nums) is always 5.

choices.text: A random number between 1 and 10
choices.explanation: DISPLAY(LENGTH(nums)) shows the length of the list, not one of the random numbers appended to it.

choices.text: A value between 5 and 50
choices.explanation: The display is LENGTH(nums) = 5, not the sum of the random values.

choices.text: The result varies and cannot be determined
choices.explanation: While the random values vary, the list length is always exactly 5 — this is deterministic.
4 would be result after one addition of n — the loop runs n=4 times, each adding 4: 4+4+4+4=16.
mystery(4): n=4, loop runs 4 times, each adding n(4): result = 0+4+4+4+4 = 16. DISPLAY(16).
8 would be after 2 iterations (4+4) — the loop runs 4 times: result = 0+4+4+4+4 = 16.
10 = 1+2+3+4 (triangular number) — this procedure adds n to itself n times, which is n² = 4² = 16.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content:
list1 ← [1, 2, 3]
list2 ← list1
APPEND(list2, 4)
DISPLAY(LENGTH(list1))

questions.question: What is displayed? (In CB pseudocode, list assignment creates a copy.)

choices.explanation: 1 is the first element of list1 — LENGTH returns the count of elements (3), not the first element's value.
4 would be correct if the assignment created a reference — in CB pseudocode, list2 ← list1 creates an independent copy.
In CB pseudocode, list2 ← list1 creates a copy. Modifying list2 (APPEND) does not affect list1. LENGTH(list1) remains 3.

choices.text: The program causes an error
choices.explanation: There is no error — the code runs correctly and displays 3.
4 is the first element — the procedure sums all elements: 4+8+2+6=20.
6 is the last element — the procedure returns the total sum of all elements, which is 20.

choices.text: The procedure returns nothing
choices.explanation: The procedure explicitly uses RETURN(total) — it always returns the computed sum.
sumList adds all elements: 0+4=4, +8=12, +2=14, +6=20. RETURN(20), result=20, DISPLAY(20).

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which TWO of the following statements about binary search are correct? Select two answers.

choices.text: Binary search can only be applied to a sorted list
choices.explanation: Binary search works by comparing the target to the middle element and eliminating half the list — this only works correctly on a sorted list.

choices.text: Binary search examines every element in the list
choices.explanation: Linear search examines every element — binary search eliminates half the remaining elements with each comparison, making it much faster.

choices.text: Binary search is more efficient than linear search for large sorted lists
choices.explanation: Binary search finds a target in at most log₂(n) steps — far fewer than the n steps needed for linear search on a large list.

choices.text: Binary search works faster than linear search on unsorted lists
choices.explanation: Binary search requires a sorted list — it cannot be used on unsorted data. On unsorted data, only linear search is valid.
Odd numbers (MOD 2 ≠ 0): 1 (odd), 4 (even), 7 (odd), 2 (even), 9 (odd), 6 (even), 3 (odd) = 4 odd numbers.
3 would be correct only if three odd numbers existed — checking: 1, 7, 9, 3 are all odd = 4 odd numbers.
7 is the total number of elements — the procedure counts only odd ones (4).
20 would be the sum of odd numbers (1+7+9+3) — the procedure counts them, not sums them.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What is the purpose of the RETURN statement in a procedure?

choices.text: It displays a value to the screen
choices.explanation: DISPLAY() shows values on screen — RETURN sends a value back to the code that called the procedure.

choices.text: It sends a value back to the code that called the procedure and ends procedure execution
choices.explanation: RETURN(expression) ends the procedure and passes the value of expression back to the caller.

choices.text: It causes the program to restart from the beginning
choices.explanation: Restarting from the beginning is not what RETURN does — RETURN exits the current procedure and passes a value to the calling code.

choices.text: It stores a value in a variable
choices.explanation: Storing a value uses assignment (←) — RETURN passes a value out of a procedure.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What is a library in the context of programming?

choices.text: A physical building where books about programming are stored
choices.explanation: In computing, a library is a collection of pre-written code, not a physical building.

choices.text: A place where a program stores all its variables
choices.explanation: Variables are stored in memory, not a library — a library is a collection of reusable code modules.

choices.text: A collection of pre-written procedures that programmers can use without knowing the implementation details
choices.explanation: A library provides ready-made procedures — programmers use them via an API without needing to write or understand the underlying code.

choices.text: A file that contains only documentation for a program
choices.explanation: Documentation files describe code — libraries contain executable procedures that other programs can use.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following is an example of sequencing in a program?

choices.text: A program that runs a loop 10 times
choices.explanation: Running a loop is iteration — sequencing is executing instructions one after another in the order they appear.

choices.text: A program that chooses between two different actions based on a condition
choices.explanation: Choosing based on a condition is selection (IF/ELSE) — not sequencing.

choices.text: A program that calls different functions depending on user input
choices.explanation: Calling different functions based on input is selection — not sequencing.

choices.text: A program that reads a value, calculates the square, then displays the result — in that order
choices.explanation: Executing three steps in order (read → calculate → display) without repetition or branching is sequencing.

choices.text: yes
choices.explanation: a=3, b=5: a > b is (3>5) = FALSE. NOT(FALSE) = TRUE. The IF block executes and displays 'yes'.

choices.text: no
choices.explanation: 'no' displays when NOT(a>b) is FALSE — since 3>5 is FALSE, NOT(FALSE) is TRUE, so 'yes' displays.

choices.text: yes no
choices.explanation: Only one branch of an IF/ELSE executes — since the condition is TRUE, only 'yes' is displayed.

choices.text: false
choices.explanation: 'false' is the value of (a>b) before NOT is applied — DISPLAY shows 'yes' based on the full condition evaluation.

stimulus.content:
PROCEDURE swap(lst, i, j)
{
   temp ← lst[i]
   lst[i] ← lst[j]
   lst[j] ← temp
   RETURN (lst)
}
myList ← [5, 3, 8, 1]
myList ← swap(myList, 1, 3)
DISPLAY(myList[1])

choices.explanation: 5 was at index 1 before the swap — swap(myList, 1, 3) exchanges elements at index 1 and 3, so 5 moves to index 3 and 8 moves to index 1.
swap exchanges positions 1 and 3: myList[1]=5 and myList[3]=8 are swapped. myList becomes [8,3,5,1]. myList[1]=8.
3 is at index 2 and is not involved in the swap — myList[2] remains 3, but myList[1] becomes 8.
1 is at index 4 and is not swapped — the swap is between indices 1 and 3, so myList[1] becomes the original value at index 3, which is 8.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: A programmer writes a simulation to model traffic flow in a city. Which of the following is a limitation of this simulation?

choices.text: Simulations can only model natural phenomena, not human behavior like traffic
choices.explanation: Simulations can model any system including human behavior, traffic, economics, and social dynamics.

choices.text: Simulations run faster than real-world events, making them useless for long-term planning
choices.explanation: Running faster is actually a benefit of simulation — it allows studying long-term scenarios quickly.

choices.text: The simulation results may not perfectly match real-world outcomes because the model simplifies real-world complexities
choices.explanation: All simulations rely on simplifying assumptions — they cannot capture every real-world variable, so results are approximations, not perfect predictions.

choices.text: Simulations must use real programming languages and cannot use pseudocode
choices.explanation: Simulations can be implemented in any language — this is not a limitation of simulation as a concept.
3 is fibonacci(4) — fibonacci(5) = fibonacci(4) + fibonacci(3) = 3 + 2 = 5.
4 is not a Fibonacci number — fibonacci(5) = 5 (sequence: 0,1,1,2,3,5).
8 is fibonacci(6) — fibonacci(5) = 5.
fibonacci(5) = fibonacci(4)+fibonacci(3) = (fibonacci(3)+fibonacci(2)) + (fibonacci(2)+fibonacci(1)) = (2+1)+(1+1) = 3+2 = 5.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following best describes an algorithm that runs in 'reasonable time'?

choices.text: An algorithm whose number of steps grows as a polynomial function of the input size
choices.explanation: Reasonable time means the algorithm's step count is bounded by a polynomial of n (like n, n², n³) — these algorithms remain practical for large inputs.

choices.text: An algorithm that always finishes in under 1 second
choices.explanation: Reasonable time is not defined by a specific time limit — it refers to algorithms whose step count grows polynomially with input size.

choices.text: An algorithm that uses the minimum possible number of steps
choices.explanation: Optimal algorithms are not necessarily the definition of reasonable time — the key is polynomial vs. exponential growth.

choices.text: An algorithm that has no loops
choices.explanation: Algorithms without loops are typically O(1) or O(n) — having loops does not prevent an algorithm from running in reasonable (polynomial) time.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What is the halting problem, and why is it significant?

choices.text: It is a problem about stopping an infinite loop — it is solved by adding REPEAT UNTIL(false)
choices.explanation: The halting problem is about whether any arbitrary program will stop — it is not solved by adding a loop condition.

choices.text: It is the problem of determining whether any given program with any given input will eventually stop — it is undecidable, meaning no algorithm can always answer it correctly
choices.explanation: The halting problem asks: given any program and input, will the program halt? Alan Turing proved no algorithm can correctly answer this for all possible programs — it is the classic undecidable problem.

choices.text: It is a problem that is hard to solve but has been fully solved using heuristics
choices.explanation: The halting problem is not just hard — it is provably undecidable. No algorithm can always give the correct answer.

choices.text: It is a problem about computer hardware overheating and stopping unexpectedly
choices.explanation: The halting problem is a theoretical computer science concept about algorithm decidability — it has nothing to do with hardware overheating.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What does this algorithm do and what does it display?

choices.text: It finds the minimum value and displays 1
choices.explanation: The algorithm replaces result when item > result — this finds the maximum, not the minimum.

choices.text: It calculates the sum of all values and displays 45
choices.explanation: The algorithm replaces result, it does not accumulate a sum — it finds the maximum element.

choices.text: It finds the maximum value and displays 9
choices.explanation: Starting with result=4, the loop replaces result whenever a larger value is found: 4→7→9. 9 is the maximum. DISPLAY(9).

choices.text: It finds the last element and displays 6
choices.explanation: The last element is 6, but the algorithm finds the maximum — 9 is larger than 6, so result=9 at the end.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What does INSERT(myList, 2, 99) do to myList = [10, 20, 30]?

choices.text: Adds 99 to the end of the list
choices.explanation: Adding to the end uses APPEND — INSERT places the new value at the specified index position.

choices.text: Replaces the element at index 2 with 99
choices.explanation: INSERT inserts a new element — it does not replace. After INSERT, the list has one more element.

choices.text: Removes the element at index 2
choices.explanation: Removing an element uses REMOVE — INSERT adds a new element at the specified index.

choices.text: Inserts 99 at index 2, shifting existing elements right — result: [10, 99, 20, 30]
choices.explanation: INSERT(list, 2, 99) inserts 99 at position 2 and shifts elements at positions 2 onward one position right.

choices.text: true
choices.explanation: x=4 > 0 is TRUE. In OR, if either operand is TRUE, the result is TRUE. TRUE OR FALSE = TRUE, so 'true' is displayed.

choices.text: false
choices.explanation: 'false' displays when both conditions are FALSE — but x>0 is TRUE, making the OR expression TRUE.

choices.text: true false
choices.explanation: Only one branch of IF/ELSE executes — since the OR condition is TRUE, only 'true' is displayed.

choices.text: Nothing is displayed
choices.explanation: The OR condition evaluates to TRUE (because x>0 is TRUE) — 'true' is displayed.

choices.text: false
choices.explanation: 7 is a prime number — none of 2, 3 divide 7 evenly, so the loop exits without returning false.

choices.text: true
choices.explanation: n=7. i starts at 2. Check 2: 7 MOD 2=1≠0. Check 3: 7 MOD 3=1≠0. i=3, 3*3=9>7, loop stops. RETURN(true). 7 is prime.
The procedure returns a Boolean (true/false), not the input number.
2 is the starting value of i inside the procedure — the procedure returns a Boolean, not i's value.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: A programmer wants to simulate flipping a fair coin 100 times and count the number of heads. Which function would they use in College Board pseudocode to simulate randomness?

choices.text: DISPLAY(0 or 1)
choices.explanation: DISPLAY shows output — it does not generate random values. RANDOM() is needed for randomness.

choices.text: MOD 2
choices.explanation: MOD 2 computes remainders — it produces a deterministic result, not a random one.

choices.text: RANDOM(0, 1)
choices.explanation: RANDOM(0, 1) returns either 0 or 1 with equal probability — this perfectly simulates a fair coin flip where one value represents heads and the other tails.

choices.text: LENGTH(coinList)
choices.explanation: LENGTH returns list size — it does not generate random values.
Only counting items divisible by 3: 3 MOD 3=0 (yes), 1(no), 4(no), 1(no), 5(no), 9 MOD 3=0 (yes), 2(no), 6 MOD 3=0 (yes) = 3 items.
2 would miss 6 — 6 MOD 3 = 0, so 6 also qualifies. The count is 3.
8 is the total number of items — the program counts only those divisible by 3.
Items divisible by 3 (n MOD 3 = 0): 3 (yes), 9 (yes), 6 (yes) = 3 items. DISPLAY(3).

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following best describes why using procedures (functions) improves a program?

choices.text: Procedures allow code to be written once and reused multiple times, reducing duplication
choices.explanation: Writing a task as a procedure allows calling it from multiple places — changes only need to be made once, and code is shorter and more maintainable.

choices.text: Procedures make the program run faster
choices.explanation: Procedures do not necessarily improve runtime speed — they improve code organization, reusability, and maintainability.

choices.text: Procedures eliminate the need for variables in a program
choices.explanation: Procedures use and contain variables — they do not eliminate the need for them.

choices.text: Procedures prevent all logic errors from occurring
choices.explanation: Procedures improve code structure but do not prevent logic errors — errors can still exist inside procedure bodies.

choices.text: even
choices.explanation: 7 MOD 2 = 1, which is not 0, so the condition is FALSE — 'odd' displays.

choices.text: odd
choices.explanation: 7 is odd: 7 MOD 2 = 1, which ≠ 0. The IF condition is FALSE, ELSE executes, displaying 'odd'.
7 is the value of x — the program displays a word ('odd') based on the condition.
1 is the value of 7 MOD 2, but it is only used as a condition — the DISPLAY outputs 'odd'.

stimulus.content:
list ← [5, 3, 8, 1, 9, 2]
INSERT(list, 3, 7)
DISPLAY(list[3])

choices.explanation: 8 was at index 3 before INSERT — after INSERT(list, 3, 7), 7 is placed at index 3 and 8 shifts to index 4.
3 is at index 2 and is not affected by INSERT at index 3 — list[3] becomes 7.
INSERT(list, 3, 7) inserts 7 at index 3. The list becomes [5,3,7,8,1,9,2]. list[3] is 7.
9 was at index 5 before INSERT — after insertion, it shifts to index 6. list[3] is the newly inserted 7.
5 is the input — mystery(5) computes the sum of integers 1 through 5: 1+2+3+4+5=15.
10 = 1+2+3+4 — the loop includes i=5 as well (continues until i > 5, so i=5 is included). Total = 15.
25 = 5² — the procedure sums integers 1 through n, not n², giving 15.
The loop adds i from 1 to n: 1+2+3+4+5=15. i reaches 6 > 5, loop stops. RETURN(15). DISPLAY(15).

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: A simulation of a weather forecasting system consistently gives inaccurate predictions for coastal areas. Which of the following is the most likely reason?

choices.text: The simulation's model does not adequately account for the unique atmospheric conditions of coastal areas
choices.explanation: Simulation inaccuracy typically results from incomplete or incorrect models — if coastal atmospheric factors aren't properly modeled, the simulation produces inaccurate predictions for those areas.

choices.text: The simulation uses a random number generator, which causes unpredictable errors
choices.explanation: Random number generators are used intentionally in simulations — they do not by themselves cause systematic prediction errors.

choices.text: The simulation runs too quickly and should be slowed down for accuracy
choices.explanation: Simulation speed does not determine accuracy — the quality of the underlying model determines prediction accuracy.

choices.text: Coastal weather is undecidable and no algorithm can predict it
choices.explanation: Undecidable problems are problems with no possible algorithmic solution — weather prediction is difficult but not undecidable. The issue is model accuracy.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What is the difference between a parameter and an argument in a procedure?

choices.text: Parameters are used in loops; arguments are used in IF statements
choices.explanation: Parameters and arguments are both related to procedures, not specific to loops or conditionals.

choices.text: A parameter is the variable in the procedure definition; an argument is the actual value passed when calling the procedure
choices.explanation: In PROCEDURE name(parameter), the listed variable is the parameter. When calling name(argument), the passed value is the argument.

choices.text: Parameters and arguments are the same thing with different names
choices.explanation: They play different roles: parameter is a placeholder in the definition; argument is the concrete value supplied during a call.

choices.text: Arguments are only used in procedures that return values; parameters are for procedures without return values
choices.explanation: Both parameters and arguments are used in all procedures — the presence of a return value is unrelated to this distinction.

choices.text: true
choices.explanation: The program does not display the word 'true' — it displays 'yes' or 'no' based on the condition.

choices.text: no
choices.explanation: 'no' displays when the condition is FALSE — a AND NOT b = true AND true = true, so 'yes' displays.

choices.text: yes
choices.explanation: a=true, NOT b = NOT false = true. true AND true = true. The IF block executes: DISPLAY('yes').

choices.text: false
choices.explanation: The program does not display 'false' — the condition evaluates to TRUE, so 'yes' is displayed.

stimulus.content:
PROCEDURE reverseList(lst)
{
   n ← LENGTH(lst)
   reversed ← []
   i ← n
   REPEAT UNTIL (i < 1)
   {
      APPEND(reversed, lst[i])
      i ← i - 1
   }
   RETURN (reversed)
}
result ← reverseList([1, 2, 3, 4])
DISPLAY(result[1])

choices.explanation: 1 is the first element of the original list — reverseList reverses it, making the first element of the reversed list 4.
2 is at index 2 in the original list — after reversal, result[1] is 4 (was last in original).
3 is at index 3 in the original — result[1] after reversal is 4.
reverseList starts at i=4 (LENGTH=4) and appends lst[4]=4, lst[3]=3, lst[2]=2, lst[1]=1. reversed=[4,3,2,1]. result[1]=4.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: An API provides a procedure called getWeather(city) that returns the current weather. A programmer calls it in their app. Which concept does this best illustrate?

choices.text: Using a library/API through procedural abstraction — the programmer uses the procedure without needing to implement the weather lookup
choices.explanation: This is procedural abstraction via an API — the programmer calls getWeather(city) and uses the result without implementing the weather data retrieval.

choices.text: The programmer must understand all the code inside getWeather to use it
choices.explanation: The point of an API is that users do NOT need to know the implementation — only the interface (what parameters it takes and what it returns).

choices.text: The programmer is performing binary search on weather data
choices.explanation: Using an API to retrieve data has nothing to do with binary search.

choices.text: The programmer is demonstrating overflow errors
choices.explanation: Overflow involves numeric values exceeding bit capacity — calling a weather API has nothing to do with overflow.
5 is less than 12 and is not added to above — 'above' contains [20, 15, 25].
Elements > 12: 20 (above[1]), 15 (above[2]), 25 (above[3]). above[2] = 15.
20 is at above[1] — above[2] is 15 (the second element appended to above).
25 is at above[3] — above[2] is 15.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What does a FOR EACH loop do?

choices.text: Runs a block of code a fixed number of times specified by the programmer
choices.explanation: Running a fixed number of times is REPEAT n TIMES — FOR EACH iterates through all elements of a list.

choices.text: Runs a block of code until a condition becomes true
choices.explanation: Running until a condition is true is REPEAT UNTIL — FOR EACH automatically iterates through list elements.

choices.text: Iterates through every element of a list, executing the body once per element
choices.explanation: FOR EACH item IN list assigns each element to item in sequence and executes the body once per element.

choices.text: Loops forever unless manually stopped
choices.explanation: FOR EACH terminates automatically after visiting all elements — it does not loop forever.

stimulus.content:
x ← 15
y ← 4
DISPLAY(x MOD y)

choices.explanation: 0 would mean 15 is divisible by 4 — 15/4 = 3 remainder 3, not 0.
4 is the divisor — MOD returns the remainder (3), not the divisor.
1 would be the remainder for 15 MOD 7 or similar — 15 MOD 4 = 3.
15 divided by 4 is 3 with remainder 3 (4×3=12, 15-12=3). 15 MOD 4 = 3.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which TWO of the following are advantages of using a simulation over testing a real-world system? Select two answers.

choices.text: Simulations always produce perfectly accurate results
choices.explanation: Simulations always involve simplifying assumptions — they are approximations, not perfect replicas of reality.

choices.text: Simulations can model dangerous scenarios (like car crashes) without real-world risk
choices.explanation: Simulating dangerous scenarios lets researchers study them safely — no people, vehicles, or environments are harmed.

choices.text: Simulations can run many years of scenarios in seconds, enabling rapid testing of multiple conditions
choices.explanation: Computer simulations can compress time dramatically — testing 50 years of climate scenarios in minutes is impossible in the real world.

choices.text: Simulations remove the need to understand the system being modeled
choices.explanation: Simulations must be built on accurate models of the system — without understanding the system, the simulation will produce meaningless results.
buildEvenList(10) creates [2,4,6,8,10] (even numbers from 2 to 10). result[3] = 6.
4 is result[2] — the list is [2, 4, 6, 8, 10], so result[3] is 6.
8 is result[4] — result[3] is 6.
10 is result[5] (the last element) — result[3] is 6.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: A programmer wants to find the value 'cat' in a sorted list of animal names using the most efficient method. What algorithm should they use?

choices.text: Linear search, because it works on all lists
choices.explanation: While linear search works on all lists, the problem specifies a sorted list — binary search would be more efficient.

choices.text: Binary search, because the list is sorted and binary search is more efficient for large sorted lists
choices.explanation: Binary search works on sorted lists and finds targets much faster than linear search by eliminating half the remaining elements each step.

choices.text: RANDOM search, checking random positions until the value is found
choices.explanation: Random search is highly inefficient and not a standard search algorithm — binary search on a sorted list is the correct approach.

choices.text: A FOR EACH loop that always starts from the last element
choices.explanation: Starting from the last element would still be linear search — binary search is more efficient for sorted data.
6 = triple(2) — that is the first element of the result. The second element is triple(4) = 12.
18 = triple(6) = result[3] — the question asks for result[2] which is 12.
applyToAll applies triple to each element: [triple(2), triple(4), triple(6)] = [6, 12, 18]. Index 2 is 12.
4 is the second element of the input list, not the output — the output at index 2 is triple(4) = 12.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What is data abstraction in programming?

choices.text: Compressing data to reduce file size
choices.explanation: Data compression is a storage technique — data abstraction is a programming design concept.

choices.text: Converting data from one format to another
choices.explanation: Data conversion is a transformation — data abstraction is about organizing and hiding complexity through higher-level structures.

choices.text: Using variables with abstract or unclear names
choices.explanation: Using unclear variable names is poor practice — data abstraction is about organizing data to manage complexity.

choices.text: Hiding implementation details and representing data at a higher level of organization
choices.explanation: Data abstraction uses structures like lists to group related data under a single name, hiding how they are stored and allowing programmers to work at a higher level.

stimulus.content:
x ← 6
y ← 4
z ← x * y + x MOD y
DISPLAY(z)

choices.explanation: z = x*y + x MOD y = 6*4 + 6 MOD 4 = 24 + 2 = 26. DISPLAY(26).
24 = 6*4 alone, but the expression also adds x MOD y = 6 MOD 4 = 2. z = 24 + 2 = 26.
2 = x MOD y = 6 MOD 4 — but z also adds x*y=24, giving z=26.
60 would result from a different combination — the correct calculation is 24+2=26.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which TWO of the following are examples of undecidable problems? Select two answers.

choices.text: Determining whether a given program will halt or run forever for all possible inputs
choices.explanation: This is the halting problem — proven undecidable by Alan Turing. No algorithm can always correctly determine this for all program-input pairs.

choices.text: Sorting a list of numbers in ascending order
choices.explanation: Sorting is a decidable, well-studied problem with many efficient algorithms (like merge sort and quicksort) — it is not undecidable.

choices.text: Determining whether any given program's output will ever contain the word 'hello' for some input
choices.explanation: This is related to the halting problem — determining arbitrary program output properties for all possible inputs is undecidable (Rice's theorem).

choices.text: Finding the maximum value in a list of numbers
choices.explanation: Finding a list maximum is a solvable algorithmic problem — it can be done in linear time and is not undecidable.
10 is the sum 1+2+3+4 — factorial multiplies: 4!=4×3×2×1=24.
factorial(4)=4×factorial(3)=4×(3×factorial(2))=4×3×(2×factorial(1))=4×3×2×1=24.
4 is the input — factorial(4) computes the product 4×3×2×1=24.

choices.text: Removes 5 from myList
choices.explanation: REMOVE removes elements — APPEND adds 5 to the end of myList.

choices.text: Inserts 5 at position 1 of myList
choices.explanation: Inserting at a specific position uses INSERT — APPEND always adds to the end.

choices.text: Adds 5 to the end of myList
choices.explanation: APPEND(list, value) adds value to the end of the list, increasing its length by 1.

choices.text: Replaces the first element with 5
choices.explanation: Replacing uses assignment (myList[1] ← 5) — APPEND adds to the end without removing anything.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What sequence of values is displayed?

choices.explanation: This would be an ascending count — the variable starts at 5 and decrements, producing a descending sequence.
This would require starting at 0 and incrementing — the code starts at 5 and decrements.
0 is not displayed — the loop stops when count=0 (condition count=0 becomes TRUE), so the loop body does not execute with count=0.
count starts at 5, displays 5, then decrements: 5→4→3→2→1. When count=0, the condition is TRUE and the loop stops.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: An algorithm that sorts n items takes n² steps. Another algorithm sorts n items in n × log(n) steps. For large values of n, which statement is most accurate?

choices.text: The n×log(n) algorithm is more efficient because it takes fewer steps for large n
choices.explanation: For any large n, n×log(n) << n², so the n×log(n) algorithm takes far fewer steps and is more efficient.

choices.text: The n² algorithm is faster because squaring is simpler than logarithms
choices.explanation: Mathematical simplicity doesn't determine speed — for large n, n² grows much faster than n×log(n), making the n×log(n) algorithm faster.

choices.text: Both algorithms are equally efficient because they both run in polynomial time
choices.explanation: Both are polynomial, but n×log(n) grows slower than n² — for large inputs, the n×log(n) algorithm is significantly faster.

choices.text: The n² algorithm is more efficient for small n but slower for large n — they are equally efficient overall
choices.explanation: While the n² algorithm may be comparable for very small n, they are not 'equally efficient overall' — algorithmic efficiency is evaluated for large inputs.

stimulus.content:
a ← 1
b ← 2
c ← 3
b ← c + a
a ← b - 1
DISPLAY(a)

choices.explanation: 1 is a's initial value — after b←c+a=4, then a←b-1=3.
Step 1: a=1,b=2,c=3. Step 2: b←c+a=3+1=4. Step 3: a←b-1=4-1=3. DISPLAY(3).
2 is b's initial value — after assignments: b becomes 4, then a becomes 4-1=3.
4 is b's value after step 2 — then a becomes b-1=4-1=3.
9 is the target value — the procedure returns a Boolean (true/false), not the target itself.

choices.text: false
choices.explanation: false would display if 9 were not in the list — 9 is the fourth element, so the procedure returns true.

choices.text: true
choices.explanation: The procedure searches for 9 in [3,7,2,9,5]. When item=9 matches target=9, RETURN(true) executes immediately. DISPLAY(true).
4 would be the index where 9 is found — the procedure returns true/false, not the index.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What is the difference between REPEAT n TIMES and REPEAT UNTIL(condition)?

choices.text: REPEAT n TIMES can only be used with numbers; REPEAT UNTIL can only be used with text
choices.explanation: Both loops can be used regardless of the data type — the difference is how they determine when to stop.

choices.text: REPEAT UNTIL is faster than REPEAT n TIMES
choices.explanation: Neither loop type is inherently faster — performance depends on what the loop body does and how many iterations run.

choices.text: They are identical — just different syntax for the same operation
choices.explanation: These are functionally different — REPEAT n TIMES has a predetermined count; REPEAT UNTIL terminates based on a condition that may require a variable number of iterations.

choices.text: REPEAT n TIMES runs a fixed number of iterations; REPEAT UNTIL runs until a condition becomes true
choices.explanation: REPEAT n TIMES always executes exactly n times; REPEAT UNTIL continues until its condition evaluates to true, which may vary each run.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What sequence of values is displayed?

choices.explanation: item*item for each element: 1²=1, 2²=4, 3²=9, 4²=16, 5²=25. Values displayed: 1 4 9 16 25.
1 2 3 4 5 would display without the multiplication — the loop displays item * item (the square of each).
This would require iterating in reverse order — FOR EACH processes elements in list order (forward).
These would be double the values (item*2) — the loop computes item*item (squares), not item*2.
7 is the total number of elements in the input — compress removes zeros, leaving only 4 non-zero elements.
compress removes zeros. Non-zero elements of [3,0,7,0,0,4,1]: 3, 7, 4, 1 = 4 elements. LENGTH(out)=4.
3 is the number of zeros in the input — the procedure keeps non-zero elements (4 of them), not removes them.
15 = 3+7+4+1 — the sum of non-zero values. LENGTH returns the count (4), not the sum.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: A programmer writes a program to check if a number is divisible by both 3 AND 5. Which condition should they use?

choices.text: IF (n MOD 3 = 0 OR n MOD 5 = 0)
choices.explanation: OR is true when either condition is true — to require BOTH divisibility conditions, AND must be used.

choices.text: IF (n MOD 15 = 1)
choices.explanation: n MOD 15 = 0 (not 1) would check divisibility by 15, which is equivalent to divisibility by both 3 and 5.

choices.text: IF (n MOD 3 = 0 AND n MOD 5 = 0)
choices.explanation: AND requires both conditions to be true simultaneously — both n MOD 3 = 0 (divisible by 3) AND n MOD 5 = 0 (divisible by 5) must be true.

choices.text: IF (n = 3 AND n = 5)
choices.explanation: n cannot equal both 3 and 5 simultaneously — the condition should check divisibility (MOD = 0), not equality.
5 is the initial value of m — the loop finds larger values: 9 > 5, so m becomes 9.
1 is the minimum value — the algorithm finds the maximum element.
7 is the last element but not the maximum — 9 > 7, so the maximum is 9.
m starts as 5. Loop: lst[2]=3<5(no change), lst[3]=9>5(m=9), lst[4]=1<9(no change), lst[5]=7<9(no change). RETURN(9). DISPLAY(9).

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What is the three fundamental building blocks of any algorithm?

choices.text: Sequencing, selection, and iteration
choices.explanation: Any algorithm can be expressed using these three: sequencing (in-order steps), selection (conditional branches), and iteration (loops).

choices.text: Variables, lists, and procedures
choices.explanation: Variables, lists, and procedures are programming constructs — the three algorithmic building blocks are sequencing, selection, and iteration.

choices.text: Input, process, output
choices.explanation: Input/process/output describes a program's structure — the three algorithmic building blocks are sequencing, selection, and iteration.

choices.text: Adding, subtracting, and comparing
choices.explanation: These are mathematical operations — the three building blocks of all algorithms are sequencing, selection, and iteration.

choices.text: other
choices.explanation: 'other' displays when 10 MOD 3 ≠ 1 — but 10 MOD 3 = 1, so the IF block executes.

choices.text: remainder one
choices.explanation: 10 MOD 3 = 1 (3×3=9, 10-9=1). The condition is TRUE, so 'remainder one' is displayed.
1 is the MOD result, but the DISPLAY shows the string 'remainder one', not the number 1.
3 is the divisor — the program displays a string based on the condition result.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following BEST explains why heuristic approaches are used to solve some problems?

choices.text: Heuristics always find the mathematically optimal solution faster
choices.explanation: Heuristics find good-enough solutions — they do not guarantee the optimal solution, which is why they are used when optimal solutions are impractical.

choices.text: Heuristics only apply to sorting problems
choices.explanation: Heuristics are applicable to many problem types — routing, scheduling, optimization, AI — not just sorting.

choices.text: Heuristics are used when the optimal algorithm would take an impractical amount of time due to exponential growth in steps
choices.explanation: For some problems, the only known algorithms that find the optimal solution require exponential time — heuristics trade optimality for speed, giving a good solution in reasonable time.

choices.text: Heuristics always produce wrong answers and are only used for demonstrations
choices.explanation: Heuristics produce useful approximations — they are widely used in real-world applications like GPS routing and game AI.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What sequence of values is displayed?

choices.explanation: 0 is not displayed — after n=1: n becomes 1-3=-2, which is ≤0, so the loop stops without displaying -2 or 0.
The loop continues as long as n>0 — after n=4: 4>0, display 4, n=1. 1>0, display 1, n=-2. -2≤0, stop.
-2 is not displayed — the condition n≤0 is checked BEFORE displaying, stopping the loop when n becomes -2.
n starts at 10. Display 10, n=7. Display 7, n=4. Display 4, n=1. Display 1, n=-2. -2≤0, loop stops. Displayed: 10 7 4 1.
buildSquares(6) creates [1,4,9,16,25,36] (squares of 1–6). result[4] = 4² = 16.
9 = 3² = result[3] — result[4] is 4² = 16.
25 = 5² = result[5] — result[4] is 4² = 16.
4 is the index value — result[4] is the value at that index, which is 4² = 16.
